I get it, but I don't know, that's just...
turns.zh: 如果让我来论证，我觉得 Meta 的核心能力是你能发现世界上的产品，你有好主意，你付诸实践，你发现有趣的产品，而你，Mark，不是那种想用任何东西定义自己的人。你想掌控一大堆好牌，最大化你的自由度，看清世界走向，然后拥有最好的飞船去那里航行。似乎我会选择一个几乎不把我限制在特定未来的品牌。我可能会寻找更像这样的东西：看，我想最大化我的机动性。我理解，但我不确定，这只是……

turns.en: I get it. But we align around a vision and a mission of what we're trying to do, and we run towards it. That's always been how we've operated. And in many ways, doing what I just suggested would kind of be running... it's like, well, we don't believe in it that much, and you're like, no. I mean, we're a company that puts a flag down around what we're doing and we're going to go do it. It's like put a wall in front of us, there's going to be a Mark-shaped hole in the wall.
turns.zh: 我理解。但我们围绕我们想做的愿景和使命保持一致，我们奔向它。我们一直是这样运作的。在很多方面，做我刚才建议的事情有点像逃跑……就像，好吧，我们没那么相信它，然后你说，不。我的意思是，我们是一家在我们所做的事情上插旗并去做的公司。就像在我们面前放一堵墙，墙上会有一个 Mark 形状的洞。

turns.en: Speaking of lightning rounds and Mark-shaped holes, you are accelerating what used to be your annual challenges. I always — when we were all kids and we didn't know each other, I was so inspired you would do your annual challenges, you would post about them, and I was like, wow, that's pretty damn cool. And then we all get a little older and we all have kids on the stage now and we all have companies on the stage now, some large, some small. The demands on your time — for me especially, I think lots of people — that space gets sucked, and you have expanded it. How? What do you mean?
turns.zh: 说到闪电问答和 Mark 形状的洞，你正在加速你以前的年度挑战。我一直——当我们都是孩子、彼此还不认识时，你每年做年度挑战并发布出来，我深受启发，觉得那太酷了。然后我们都长大了一点，现在我们在台上都有孩子，都有公司，有些大有些小。时间上的需求——尤其对我而言，我想对很多人来说——那个空间被吸走了，而你却扩大了它。怎么说？

turns.en: Well, you used to do annual challenges and I feel like you're now doing weekly challenges. You're designing t-shirts, you're making sculptures. I'm just trying to do inspiring things. I don't know. I'm also really competitive.
turns.zh: 嗯，你以前做年度挑战，我觉得你现在在做每周挑战。你在设计 T 恤，你在做雕塑。我只是想做一些鼓舞人心的事情。我不知道。我也很有竞争心。

turns.en: Who's your competition for this? What do you mean?
turns.zh: 你的竞争对手是谁？什么意思？

turns.en: Oh, I was just thinking about other things that I'm doing. I'm like, what have I started doing? I got into all these more extreme sports and fighting and stuff. I don't know. I mean, we face a lot of competition in a lot of different aspects of what we do. There's the social media competitors, there's the platform competitors. I think Apple is a bigger competitor than people realize. They kind of think, hey, they're doing a different type of thing, but I don't know. I think over the next 10, 15 years, I think that kind of battle — ideological battle over what should the architecture be of the next set of platforms. Are they going to be the closed integrated Apple model that Apple has always done? Which again, I mean, there are multiple good ways to build things. If you look at the different generations of computing, PCs, mobile, they've all had sort of a closed integrated version and an open version. The thing that I think there's just a ton of recency bias around is because iPhone basically won — I know there are more Android phones out there, but iPhone is sort of the intellectual leader and by far has all the... let's take it as a given. I think there's the recency bias and probably almost everyone here has an iPhone. Because of the recency bias, there's this view that, oh, this is just the superior way to do things. But I don't actually think that's a given. In the PC era, Windows with the open ecosystem was the leader. Part of my goal for the next 10, 15 years, the next generation of platforms, is to build the next generation of open platforms and have the open platforms win. I think that's going to lead to a much more vibrant tech industry. Now there are advantages of doing a closed integrated model. I think Apple will have a place for sure. I expect them to be our primary competitor, and I think it will not be just a product competition. I think it's in some ways very deeply values-driven and ideological competition around what the future of the tech industry should be, and how open these platforms — whether it's things like Llama and AI, or the glasses or different things — should be for developers. An individual someone getting started in their dorm room like me to not have to ask for permission to go build the next set of awesome things.
turns.zh: 哦，我只是在想我正在做的其他事情。我就像，我开始做什么了？我参与了所有这些更极限的运动和格斗之类的东西。我不知道。我的意思是，我们在所做的很多方面都面临很多竞争。有社交媒体竞争对手，有平台竞争对手。我认为苹果是比人们意识到的更大的竞争对手。他们觉得，嘿，他们在做不同类型的事情，但我不确定。我认为在未来 10 到 15 年，那种战斗——关于下一代平台架构应该是什么样的意识形态之战。它们会是苹果一直做的封闭集成模式吗？再说一次，有多种好的构建方式。如果你看看不同代的计算，PC、移动，它们都有某种封闭集成版本和开放版本。我认为存在大量近因偏差的事情是，因为 iPhone 基本上赢了——我知道外面有更多 Android 手机，但 iPhone 是某种智力领导者，并且远远拥有所有……我们姑且认为这是事实。我认为存在近因偏差，可能这里几乎每个人都有 iPhone。由于近因偏差，有一种观点认为，哦，这只是做事的优越方式。但我实际上不认为这是理所当然的。在 PC 时代，拥有开放生态系统的 Windows 是领导者。我未来 10 到 15 年、下一代平台的部分目标是构建下一代开放平台，并让开放平台获胜。我认为这将带来一个更有活力的科技行业。现在做封闭集成模式有优势。我认为苹果肯定会有位置。我预计他们会成为我们的主要竞争对手，我认为这不仅仅是产品竞争。我认为这在某些方面是深深植根于价值观和意识形态的竞争，关于科技行业的未来应该是什么，以及这些平台——无论是像 Llama 和 AI，还是眼镜或其他东西——应该对开发者有多开放。一个像我一样在宿舍里起步的个人，不必请求许可就能去构建下一波很棒的东西。

turns.en: I've got a closing question here. Please, thank you so much. We have a lot of builders in the audience tonight, a lot of founders. We're in probably the most...
turns.zh: 我有个结束问题。非常感谢。今晚观众中有很多建设者，很多创始人。我们可能处于最……

turns.en: Interesting technology environments since the early mobile days in terms of opportunity. It's been 20 years, you might have to go back a little bit. But what advice do you have for founders today on something that's different than trying to pattern match Mark Zuckerberg from 2004, given we live in a different world today?
turns.zh: 从早期移动时代以来，技术环境在机遇方面一直很有趣。已经过去 20 年了，你可能得往回看一点。但考虑到我们今天生活在一个不同的世界，对于今天的创始人，你有什么建议——不同于试图模仿 2004 年的 Mark Zuckerberg？

turns.en: Yeah, I don't know. I mean, just do something that you care about. And if you're trying to run our strategy, try to learn as quickly as you can. But I think part of what I'm trying to say is there are different ways to build stuff, right? Our way worked for me and our team, but different things have clearly worked for other companies. I don't know. One day, my daughter and I went to a Taylor Swift concert. She was like, 'Dad, I kind of want to be like Taylor Swift when I grow up.' I was like, 'You can't, that's not available to you.' But she thought about it and said, 'All right, when I grow up, I want people to want to be like August Chan Zuckerberg.' I was like, 'Hell yeah, hell yeah.' So I think it's like, look, learn from other people's successes and failures, but do your own thing.
turns.zh: 嗯，我不知道。我的意思是，就做你关心的事情。如果你想执行我们的策略，那就尽可能快地学习。但我想说的是，构建东西有不同的方式，对吧？我们的方式对我以及我们的团队有效，但其他公司显然也有不同的成功之道。我不知道。有一天，我带女儿去看了 Taylor Swift 的演唱会。她说：‘爸爸，我长大后想成为 Taylor Swift 那样的人。’我说：‘你做不到，那不适合你。’但她想了想，然后说：‘好吧，那我长大后要让人们想成为 August Chan Zuckerberg。’我说：‘太棒了，太棒了。’所以我觉得，要学习别人的成功和失败，但要做你自己的事。

turns.en: Love that, love that. Well, that is the perfect place to leave things. We made you something that you already have a very amazing, well-designed shirt. I hope you have room in your life for more than one.
turns.zh: 说得好，说得好。嗯，这正好是结束的完美时机。我们给你做了一件你已经拥有的、非常棒的、设计精良的衬衫。我希望你的生活里还能再容纳一件。

turns.en: I do. You know, I used to only wear one type of shirt, now I've moved on.
turns.zh: 我确实有空间。你知道，我以前只穿一种衬衫，现在我已经变了。

turns.en: So David and I made you a custom one-of-one shirt that represents tonight. Thank you. It is size Zuck, so no one else can—there's no way it can ever be made again. And we've got these coordinates on the back. The first one, GPS coordinates, represents Kirkland House where you wrote the first line of code for Facebook, and the second one is Chase Center. Awesome. So thank you for joining us here tonight.
turns.zh: 所以 David 和我给你做了一件定制的、独一无二的衬衫，代表今晚。谢谢。这是 Zuck 的尺码，所以别人穿不了——再也做不出第二件了。背面有这些坐标。第一组 GPS 坐标代表 Kirkland House，你在那里写了 Facebook 的第一行代码；第二组是 Chase Center。太棒了。所以感谢你今晚来到这里。

turns.en: Thank you for joining us.
turns.zh: 谢谢你们邀请我。

turns.en: Wow, what a night. Absolutely crazy. I mean, Mark has done many interviews this year, both with other podcasts and in traditional press, but that felt different. If for no other reason than it happened live in front of a 6,000-person audience in an arena. But I wasn't expecting it to feel that different.
turns.zh: 哇，今晚太棒了。简直疯狂。我是说，Mark 今年做了很多采访，包括其他播客和传统媒体，但这次感觉不一样。光是在一个体育馆里面对 6000 名现场观众这一点就不同。但我没想到会感觉这么不同。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, it was the wildest experience of my life being up there. I don't even know what else could compare. Pretty insane.
turns.zh: 是啊，我是说，站在台上是我人生中最疯狂的体验。我甚至不知道还有什么能比得上。太不可思议了。

turns.en: Well, listeners, as you may have noticed, thanks to our sponsors, this conversation was uninterrupted. And we do want to reflect a little bit and share some of our thoughts and how we're feeling looking back on this conversation with you. But first, we do want to share a word on StatSig and Crusoe.
turns.zh: 好了，听众朋友们，你们可能已经注意到了，感谢我们的赞助商，这次对话没有中断。我们想稍微回顾一下，分享一些我们的想法以及回顾这次对话的感受。但首先，我们想介绍一下 StatSig 和 Crusoe。

turns.en: So StatSig. Mark's most famous catchphrase is probably 'move fast and break things.' But like we talked about with him, despite instilling this in Facebook's engineering culture, Facebook doesn't actually break very often. Certainly not anymore, and really relative to its peers, not even throughout its past. Facebook invested hundreds of thousands of engineering hours in a set of internal tools. These tools let any engineer set up new metrics, ship new features, and measure performance in real time. That means anyone could just ship a new feature, but they always had metrics to use as guardrails, and they could always roll back a feature if anything broke. Oh man, there are legendary stories of engineers shipping features in their first week at boot camp as interns or new hires at Facebook and Meta over the years. And you might wish that you could do the same on your team and build products like they build products at Facebook: ship fast, make data-driven decisions, iterate rapidly. But you need the right tools, so you're stuck, right? Well, enter StatSig. StatSig has built the world's first product acceleration platform, combining tools like feature flags, product analytics, experimentation, and observability all in one place, helping you move faster and make smarter decisions. Even better, StatSig was literally founded by an ex-Meta team who wanted to help everyone build like the best and bring these same tools to the market. Today, many of the world's leading tech companies rely on StatSig, including OpenAI, Microsoft, Notion, Anthropic, Figma, plus thousands of early-stage startups. David, every time we work with StatSig, this list gets more and more impressive. Like now it is purely A-list companies. It's awesome. So if you're ready to accelerate your growth and democratize product building at your company, go to statsig.com/acquired and when you get in touch, just tell them that Ben and David sent you. Thanks, StatSig.
turns.zh: 那么 StatSig。Mark 最著名的口头禅大概是‘快速行动，打破常规’。但正如我们和他聊到的，尽管这被灌输到 Facebook 的工程文化中，Facebook 实际上并不经常出问题。当然现在更是如此，而且相对于同行，即使在过去也不常出问题。Facebook 投入了数十万工程小时来开发一套内部工具。这些工具让任何工程师都能设置新指标、发布新功能并实时衡量性能。这意味着任何人都可以发布新功能，但他们总有指标作为护栏，并且如果出现问题，他们总能回滚功能。哦，天哪，多年来，Facebook 和 Meta 有无数传奇故事，关于工程师在入职第一周的新兵训练营就以实习生或新员工身份发布功能。你可能希望你的团队也能这样做，像 Facebook 那样构建产品：快速发布、基于数据做决策、快速迭代。但你需要合适的工具，所以你被困住了，对吧？那么，StatSig 来了。StatSig 构建了世界上第一个产品加速平台，将功能标志、产品分析、实验和可观测性等工具整合在一起，帮助你更快行动并做出更明智的决策。更棒的是，StatSig 正是由前 Meta 团队创立的，他们希望帮助每个人像最优秀的公司一样构建产品，并将这些工具推向市场。如今，许多世界领先的科技公司都依赖 StatSig，包括 OpenAI、Microsoft、Notion、Anthropic、Figma，以及数千家早期初创公司。David，每次我们与 StatSig 合作，这个名单都越来越令人印象深刻。现在完全是顶级公司了。太棒了。所以，如果你准备好加速增长并在公司内普及产品构建，请访问 statsig.com/acquired，联系时告诉他们 Ben 和 David 介绍的。谢谢 StatSig。

turns.en: And now for Crusoe. Crusoe is a climate-aligned cloud platform built specifically for AI workloads and powered by clean energy. They build and operate GPU data centers powered by low-cost, stranded energy that otherwise goes to waste or, worse, gets emitted as greenhouse gases. It's crazy. When Acquired first started working with Crusoe, this was a cool idea. Now they're like one of the most important companies in the world, with an AI cloud that's superior to the hyperscalers, and a whole bunch of the largest companies in the world trusting their AI infrastructure to them. Yeah, it's easy to think about AI as like, 'Oh, that's a bunch of PhDs at Meta or OpenAI or Anthropic or whatever, tinkering with model weights in their office and hitting compute.' But there's this whole other industrial side of AI: everything that happens after you press 'go' on the model training. That's energy, cooling, construction, all the physical infrastructure behind AI. And Crusoe is powering that by producing or repurposing huge amounts of power. We are talking gigawatts in their development pipeline, which is nuclear reactor amounts of power, for less cost than other providers and with zero or, in some cases, actually negative emissions. It's super important. If you listen to Zuck and others talk about what the bottleneck to AI progress is, it's actually not compute but energy. And Crusoe is solving that problem. It's just an awesome company. We are super proud to work with them and to be investors in the company. You can work with Crusoe either through their managed AI cloud, which is great for startups and enterprises who want a complete end-to-end platform for AI, or directly as a data center customer, which several of the largest companies in the world are now doing. So just go over to crusoe.ai/acquired, or click the link in the show notes, and tell them that Ben and David sent you.
turns.zh: 接下来是 Crusoe。Crusoe 是一个气候友好的云平台，专为 AI 工作负载构建，由清洁能源驱动。他们构建并运营 GPU 数据中心，使用低成本、原本会被浪费或更糟地被排放为温室气体的搁浅能源。这太疯狂了。当 Acquired 刚开始与 Crusoe 合作时，这还是一个很酷的想法。现在他们已成为世界上最重要的公司之一，其 AI 云优于超大规模云服务商，并且许多全球最大的公司都将 AI 基础设施托付给他们。是的，人们很容易认为 AI 就是‘一群 Meta、OpenAI 或 Anthropic 的博士在办公室里摆弄模型权重并运行计算’。但 AI 还有另一个工业层面：模型训练按下‘开始’之后的一切。那就是能源、冷却、建设，所有 AI 背后的物理基础设施。而 Crusoe 通过生产或再利用大量电力来支撑这一切。他们的开发管道中有千兆瓦级的电力，相当于核反应堆的发电量，成本低于其他供应商，并且零排放，在某些情况下甚至是负排放。这非常重要。如果你听 Zuck 和其他人谈论 AI 进步的瓶颈，实际上不是算力，而是能源。而 Crusoe 正在解决这个问题。这是一家非常棒的公司。我们非常自豪能与他们合作，并成为该公司的投资者。你可以通过他们的托管 AI 云与 Crusoe 合作，这对想要完整端到端 AI 平台的初创企业和企业来说非常合适；或者直接作为数据中心客户，全球几家最大的公司已经在这样做了。所以请访问 crusoe.ai/acquired，或点击节目说明中的链接，告诉他们 Ben 和 David 介绍的。

turns.en: Okay, so David, reflections on this conversation. The biggest thing that I kept thinking going into the night, as we're talking with Mark, as we're talking with his team, you know, people kept saying, 'We don't really do this. Mark doesn't really do this.' And I kept thinking, 'Yeah, he kind of does, because he's done all these podcasts this year, and he does Facebook Meta Connect, like he's done big events before, of course, right?' And he does a...
turns.zh: 好了，David，对这次对话的反思。我整晚一直在想的一件事是，当我们和 Mark 以及他的团队交谈时，人们一直说：‘我们一般不这样做。Mark 一般不这样做。’我一直在想：‘嗯，他其实做的，因为他今年做了所有这些播客，而且他做 Facebook Meta Connect，他以前也做过大型活动，当然对吧？’而且他做……

turns.en: Good number of in-person press interviews too. It's not like he doesn't talk to the traditional press, even though that has kind of become a narrative. It's not really true. However, Mark has not done an external several-thousanders live thing like this. This is a very unusual format and kind of an uncomfortable one. Even for you and I, like we're so used to stopping, starting, being thoughtful in our answers, and like this is a show you're performing. There are no breaks, there are no retakes.
turns.zh: 他还做了不少面对面的媒体采访。他并不是不跟传统媒体交流，尽管这已经成了一种说法，但其实并非如此。不过，马克从未做过这种面对几千人的外部现场活动。这是一种非常不寻常的形式，甚至有点让人不舒服。就算是你和我，我们也习惯了停顿、开始、深思熟虑地回答，而这是一场表演，没有休息，没有重来。
没错。但马克和所有 Meta 高管都非常投入。整个高管团队都来了，他们腾出了一整天，实际上我们前一天晚上也做了一些事情。很多董事会成员都在场，很多对马克重要的人也在场，他的家人也来了。他们把这件事办成了一个活动。
turns.en: Yeah, right. But Mark and all the Meta execs really embraced it. A bunch of the executive team came, they took off this whole day, and actually some stuff we did the night before too. A bunch of the board members were there, a lot of people important to Mark were there, his family came. They made it an event.
I thought this was a big deal for us. I was kind of shocked to the degree that Mark also thought it was a big deal for him, which is super cool.
turns.zh: 我以为这对我们来说是一件大事。我有点惊讶，马克也觉得这对他是一件大事，这太酷了。

turns.en: Totally agree. That's one. I was also surprised and delighted that he was willing to dive into history with us. Yes, we totally did not expect that. He's so usually maniacally focused on the future. And in our conversations to prep with him before the event, he was like, "I think of you guys as a history podcast, you know, let's talk about the future." I'm like, okay, okay, but we want to be grounded in history. And he showed up and was totally ready to go back. And I think that made the talking about the present and the future even better.
turns.zh: 完全同意。还有一点，我也很惊讶和高兴他愿意和我们深入探讨历史。是的，我们完全没想到。他通常都狂热地专注于未来。在活动前的准备对话中，他说：“我觉得你们是个历史播客，咱们聊聊未来吧。”我说，好吧好吧，但我们想以历史为基础。结果他到了现场，完全准备好回顾过去。我觉得这让关于现在和未来的讨论更加精彩了。

turns.en: Totally, because you could create these through lines. I mean, as funny as your interjection on "let's go back to the IPO moment" was, it opened up the door to have these comparative moments. Like, is what Meta doing today similar to something you've done over and over? Should we be watching for a pattern here, or are you very different today than you were historically? The way that he was talking about that stuff on stage felt very authentic, and I just haven't heard him speak in that way before, at least publicly.
turns.zh: 完全如此，因为你可以创造出这些贯穿始终的线索。我的意思是，尽管你插话说“让我们回到 IPO 时刻”很有趣，但它打开了这些对比时刻的大门。比如，Meta 今天做的事情是否与你反复做过的事情相似？我们应该留意一种模式，还是你如今与历史上截然不同？他在台上谈论这些事情的方式感觉非常真实，我以前从未听过他那样说话，至少公开场合没有。

turns.en: I think it was also a great way to let us all get a window into his psyche, which kind of brings us to another point, which is like he is still in it. Oh, what other founders of companies like that? I mean, there's Jensen. Who else? You know, it's the two of them.
turns.zh: 我认为这也是一个很好的方式，让我们都能一窥他的内心世界，这又引出了另一点，那就是他仍然全身心投入。还有哪些公司创始人像这样？我的意思是，有 Jensen。还有谁？你知道，就他们两个。

turns.en: Yeah. I think the casual observer of Meta might observe like, you know, Mark's been running it for 20 years, and most of the time these founders kind of go and do something else. They become executive chairman, or they step into a board role, or they own 4% of the company. There's some pattern there. And for Mark, I think it was plain as day on stage: he is more in it than ever. And I don't think he thinks he's like halfway through his journey. Like, I don't think he's "20 years in at 40, I'll be done." I don't get that sense either.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为对 Meta 的普通观察者可能会注意到，马克已经经营了 20 年，而大多数时候这些创始人都会去做别的事情。他们成为执行主席，或者进入董事会，或者持有公司 4% 的股份。存在某种模式。而对于马克，我认为在台上已经一目了然：他比以往任何时候都更投入。我不认为他觉得自己的旅程已经过半。我不认为他是“40 岁干了 20 年，我就结束了。”我也没有那种感觉。

turns.en: I think Meta is his vehicle by which he wants to live his entire life, and he wants to make things with this group of people that he wants to make, period. And that is kind of the product strategy.
turns.zh: 我认为 Meta 是他度过整个人生的工具，他想和这群人一起创造他想创造的东西，就是这样。这基本上就是产品策略。

turns.en: I got chills when he said the 20-year mistake, and then I got even more chills when he said "but 20 years actually isn't that long." Yeah, it's a pretty illustrative comment.
turns.zh: 当他说到“20 年的错误”时，我起了一身鸡皮疙瘩，然后当他说“但 20 年其实并不长”时，我更是起了一身鸡皮疙瘩。是的，这是一个很有启发性的评论。

turns.en: Totally. I was appreciative that he engaged with us on "be critical of the company," because honestly I was asking that as research for when we inevitably do our Meta episode. I think he gave us a regret, not a criticism. But we were live on stage in front of 6,000 people, and it's not really the right format for that.
turns.zh: 完全同意。我很感激他愿意和我们讨论“批评公司”，因为老实说，我问这个问题是为了给我们将来不可避免要做 Meta 那期节目做研究。我觉得他给了我们一个遗憾，而不是批评。但我们在 6000 人面前现场直播，这确实不是适合那种讨论的形式。

turns.en: Yep, totally. That said, obviously a very interesting answer. I think related though, back to the "he's still in it" — in some sense, Reality Labs you could look at like his Blue Origin, 100%. It's just within Meta. I'm glad you caught this too. Other big tech CEO founders have their moment running the company, they take a board role, they go do another thing, and oftentimes it's big and important for the world and capital intensive. And Mark is doing that but inside Meta with Reality Labs.
turns.zh: 是的，完全同意。话虽如此，这显然是一个非常有趣的回答。我认为这又回到了“他仍然投入”这一点——从某种意义上说，你可以把 Reality Labs 看作他的蓝色起源，100%。只是它在 Meta 内部。我很高兴你也注意到了这一点。其他大型科技公司的 CEO 创始人都有他们经营公司的时刻，然后他们担任董事会职务，去做另一件事，而且通常这件事对世界来说重大、重要且资本密集。而马克正在做这件事，但在 Meta 内部通过 Reality Labs 来做。

turns.en: I think it'll be super fascinating 20 to 50 years from now to reflect back and say, what were the unintended or perhaps intended outcomes of co-mingling multiple huge swings under one corporate umbrella, versus having people who are either CEO of multiple companies concurrently or step down from one to run the other? For Mark, I kind of feel like again, Meta is his vehicle for executing the things that he thinks are awesome products. And of course it's not just awesome products, but like things that could let him have more control over his universe. He's clearly a guy who values having a lot of degrees of freedom and doesn't like being boxed in.
turns.zh: 我认为 20 到 50 年后回顾起来会非常有趣，看看将多个巨大赌注合并到一个公司旗下，与让一个人同时担任多家公司 CEO 或从一家公司卸任去经营另一家相比，会产生哪些意料之外或意料之中的结果。对于马克，我再次感觉 Meta 是他执行他认为很棒的产品想法的工具。当然，不仅仅是很棒的产品，还有那些能让他对自己的宇宙有更多控制权的东西。他显然是一个重视自由度、不喜欢被束缚的人。

turns.en: I loved your turn-based strategy game of "get more turns, learn more on each turn." Like, oh man, Starcraft pro player 101 there. Yes. But it'll be interesting to see the knock-on effects of having Reality Labs in the Meta organization versus as a new venture.
turns.zh: 我喜欢你那个回合制策略游戏的比喻：“获得更多回合，每回合学到更多。”就像，天哪，星际争霸职业选手入门课。是的。但看看 Reality Labs 放在 Meta 组织内而不是作为一个新创企业会带来什么连锁反应，将会很有趣。

turns.en: Totally. All of that brings me to, frankly, just my biggest overwhelming takeaway from the whole experience, which is: Ben, you've developed a really great research interview question that you use on all the sources that we talk to now, which is you ask, "What is the one thing that is most misunderstood about this company, this organization, etc." And everybody at Meta for years always would say "Mark." And I never totally got it until this evening.
turns.zh: 完全同意。所有这些让我想到，坦白说，我从整个经历中得到的最大的、压倒性的收获是：Ben，你开发了一个非常好的研究采访问题，你现在用它来问我们所有的消息来源，那就是你问：“关于这家公司、这个组织，最被误解的一件事是什么？”多年来，Meta 的每个人总是说“马克”。直到今晚我才完全理解。

turns.en: He's both a singular individual himself, but it's not just that. It's that a true superpower of the company is that it is architected from top to bottom — legally, financially, organizationally, culturally — to reflect and amplify his immense strength. Which I think is probably true of Apple, Steve Jobs; Bill Gates, Microsoft; Nvidia, Jensen. Yes. But we are right up close to the ways in which Meta is a sort of amplifier, almost like it's a way for you to take the gain on Mark's output and turn it up 10,000x.
turns.zh: 他本人是一个独特的人，但不仅如此。公司真正的超能力在于，它从上到下——法律上、财务上、组织上、文化上——都被设计成反映和放大他巨大力量的样子。我认为苹果和史蒂夫·乔布斯、比尔·盖茨和微软、英伟达和 Jensen 可能也是如此。是的。但我们非常接近地看到了 Meta 在某种程度上是一个放大器，几乎就像是一种方式，让你把马克的产出增益放大一万倍。

turns.en: Yes. And I think what was so striking about it to me, versus — you're absolutely right, all those other companies — it is generally accepted in the public narrative about Apple under Steve Jobs, about Nvidia under Jensen, that that is the case. I don't think it is about Meta and Mark. I don't think people understand that. I didn't understand that until this experience.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为对我来说如此引人注目的是，与你说的完全正确——所有其他公司相比——在公众叙事中，关于史蒂夫·乔布斯领导下的苹果、关于 Jensen 领导下的英伟达，人们普遍接受这一点。但我不认为关于 Meta 和马克也是如此。我认为人们不理解这一点。直到这次经历我才理解。

turns.en: Are you saying Meta is still very much on Mark Zuckerberg production? I'll see myself out.
turns.zh: 你是说 Meta 仍然很大程度上是马克·扎克伯格的产品？我这就走。

turns.en: Well put. There we go. Well, listeners, thank you so much for joining us on this journey. Come talk about it with us in the Slack, acquired.fm's Slack. Would love to hear all of your thoughts as well. Join our email list at acquired.fm. That will let you basically know every single time a new episode drops, or when we are doing something like Chase Center again, to be the first to know about that. God, if we ever do something like that again. We've got a merch store, check it out at acquired.fm.
turns.zh: 说得好。就这样吧。好了，听众们，非常感谢你们加入我们的这段旅程。来我们的 Slack（acquired.fm 的 Slack）和我们聊聊吧。我们也很想听听你们的想法。加入我们的邮件列表，访问 acquired.fm。这样你就能第一时间知道每一集新节目上线，或者当我们再次做像 Chase Center 这样的活动时，你会第一个知道。天哪，如果我们再搞一次类似活动的话。我们还有周边商店，去 acquired.fm 看看吧。

turns.en: FM, we've got our second show where we are always interviewing earlier stage companies than Meta, but where we think there are great insightful conversations with founders and CEOs. And David, I know you've got some thank yous. One last thing, final thank yous. Thank you to Mark, thank you to basically the entire Meta executive team who helped with the evening. Thank you to Hermes for dressing us, which was my favorite Easter of the night. Yes, so fun. Thank you to Jamie Diamond, JP Morgan Chase, and JP Morgan Payments for making it all possible. It was truly a dream come true, and that is because of our incredible partnership. Indeed it was. Well listeners, we'll see you next time. Yes, in a couple of weeks with the full show. We are pumped to drop it. We'll see you next time. Who got the truth? Is it you? Is it you? Is it you who got the truth now? Huh?
turns.zh: FM，我们迎来了第二场节目，我们总是采访比 Meta 更早期的公司，但我们认为这些公司与创始人和 CEO 的对话非常有见地。David，我知道你有一些感谢要说。最后一件事，最后的感谢。感谢 Mark，感谢基本上整个 Meta 高管团队，他们协助了今晚的活动。感谢 Hermes 为我们提供服装，这是今晚我最喜欢的复活节彩蛋。是的，太有趣了。感谢 Jamie Diamond、摩根大通和摩根大通支付，让这一切成为可能。这真的是梦想成真，而这都归功于我们不可思议的合作关系。确实如此。好了，听众们，我们下次再见。是的，几周后带来完整节目。我们迫不及待要发布了。下次见。谁得到了真相？是你吗？是你吗？是你得到了真相吗？嗯？
